text: The patient is positioned in the steep reverse Trendelenburg in the right lateral decubitus position with arms outstretched. A 12mm trocar is inserted in the left upper quadrant using the Hassan technique, and the abdomen is insufflated. An 8mm robotic camera with a 30-degree angle is inserted, followed by three additional 8mm trocars along the left hemiabdomen. The peritoneum is incised lateral to the colonic reflection, and dissection proceeds through retroperitoneal fat to identify the transversus abdominis muscle. The dissection extends cranially onto the diaphragm and into the pelvis, preserving the subcostal nerve. Retroperitoneal fat is visible herniating through the posterior lumbar defect, with the hernia edges clearly defined.

The surgeon cuts and removes a stitch from the prior repair, revealing a defect measuring three by two centimeters. An attempt to close the defect with a 1-0 V-lock suture is shown, but the suture pulls through the friable tissue. A 7.5 by 15 centimeter barred soft mesh is then placed into the abdominal cavity, centered over the defect with overlap in all directions. The mesh is secured using a 2-0 Vicryl stitch and is visualized to ensure it lies completely flat in the posterior space.
The surgeon closes the peritoneum with a running two-oh V-lock suture, completing the repair. The final visualization shows the repaired area with no visible defects.
